narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 2.60 and middle-family income background, you've shown determination in pursuing law school, including planning to attend full-time like most students. The admissions model evaluated your profile to predict your chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimating a 48% probability that you would fail. This higher-than-ideal risk of failure is why your application was rejected—the school uses this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The model's decision hinged mostly on three key factors from your record, ranked by their influence. Your LSAT score of 28, which is notably below the typical 36.7 average, stood out as the strongest driver pushing the prediction toward failure, as lower scores like yours often signal challenges in bar-level reasoning. On a more positive note, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 7—better than the average 5.53—strongly pulled the prediction back toward passing, highlighting your later academic improvement. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 7, above the average 5.71, provided another helpful boost by demonstrating early consistency in your grades. Compared to typical students, your academic deciles are strengths that offset some concerns, but the LSAT gap was decisive in tipping the scales. While your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of applicants, it didn't sway the outcome much here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the average, as even a modest improvement could reshape your prediction dramatically. Strengthening any weak areas through targeted prep or additional coursework might also help in reapplying. You're clearly capable in school rankings, so building on that foundation could open doors at other programs willing to look beyond a single metric. Keep pushing—many students rebound stronger from setbacks like this.
